planting.note: Plant bulbs or transplant divisions with intact roots in early fall in moist, very rich, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; ramps require cold-stratification and are very slow from seed — planting bulb divisions is much faster, yielding harvestable plants in 2–3 years.

directions: Plant bulb divisions in early fall in moist, very rich, leaf-mold-enriched, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in partial to full shade under deciduous hardwoods — the classic habitat is slopes with sugar maple, beech, and tulip poplar; ramps are a celebrated foraged edible of eastern North American forests with broad, smooth, rich-green leaves (like lily of the valley) and a small, white, garlic-like bulb with an intense, complex garlic-wild onion flavor that has become a culinary phenomenon. Both leaves and bulbs are edible; leaves emerge in early spring before the canopy closes and die back by June. Harvest no more than one leaf per plant per year, and never take more than a third of any colony to ensure sustainable harvest. Slow-growing: colonies planted from bulb divisions become harvestable in 3–5 years and persist indefinitely in suitable shaded conditions.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed immediately after harvest in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; sweet cicely requires cold stratification for germination and will not germinate from dry or stored seed without special treatment — fresh fall sowing is essential.

directions: Direct sow fresh seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; sweet cicely is a European woodland herb with large, soft, ferny, bright-green leaves that are powerfully and pleasantly anise-scented — the most intensely anise-flavored of all edible herbs — used to flavor fish, salads, soups, and desserts. The large, ribbed, green seeds are edible when young and sweet; the taproot is also edible. The whole plant acts as a natural sweetener, potentially reducing the amount of sugar needed in cooked dishes. One of very few aromatic culinary herbs that thrives in shade rather than full sun. Self-sows generously once established. The seeds have a very short viability period and must be sown fresh in fall — purchased dry seed has very low germination success. Not recommended for hot, humid summers above zone 7.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; green-and-gold is one of the finest flowering native groundcovers for shaded sites and is particularly excellent under deciduous trees in the southeastern US.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; green-and-gold (goldenstar) is a native eastern US woodland groundcover that produces a spreading, semi-evergreen mat of textured, hairy leaves topped with cheerful, five-petaled, bright yellow flowers from early spring through late fall — an exceptionally long bloom season for a shade plant that gives this little groundcover outsized garden value. Spreads by stolons and self-seeding to fill in moderately without becoming invasive. Provides valuable spring pollen for early native bees. An outstanding, low-maintenance, deer-resistant native for the front of shaded borders, under deciduous trees, and along shaded walkways in the Southeast and Gulf South. More heat and drought tolerant than its woodland origins might suggest.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade in spring; hairy alumroot is significantly more tolerant of heat, humidity, and summer drought than most heuchera species and hybrids, making it the best heuchera for zone 7–9 gardens.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; hairy alumroot is a native Appalachian heuchera that is far superior to most cultivated heuchera hybrids for hot, humid southern climates — it tolerates summer heat, humidity, and drought that kills the popular colorful hybrids outright, while producing bold, large (4–8 inch), maple-shaped, softly hairy leaves in light green, bronze, or deep burgundy-purple, and tall, airy panicles of tiny white flowers in late summer that attract hummingbirds. The parents of many popular 'Caramel,' 'Autumn Bride,' and 'Brownies' cultivars trace back to H. villosa. Divide every 3–5 years in fall or early spring when the crown begins to heave out of the soil. Excellent long-term shade perennial for the Southeast and Gulf South.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions after the last frost threat in moist, well-drained, organically rich, slightly acidic soil in full shade to partial shade; peacock ginger goes completely dormant in winter and re-emerges reliably in spring in zones 8–11 with minimal care.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions after last frost in moist, well-drained, organically rich, slightly acidic soil in full to partial shade; peacock ginger is a Southeast Asian cousin of culinary ginger grown purely for its extraordinarily beautiful foliage — broad, flat leaves that lie close to the ground and are decorated with intricate patterns of dark-green, silver-gray, bronze, and chocolate in overlapping concentric zones, resembling the eye-pattern on a peacock feather. Small, lavender-pink flowers appear among the leaves in summer. The plant goes completely dormant in winter, disappearing below ground level. Ideal as a weed-suppressing, low groundcover in shade gardens and under tall trees in Gulf Coast and subtropical landscapes. Spreads slowly by rhizomes to form attractive colonies. Related species K. rotunda and K. elegans offer similar ornamental value.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
